title: Recursion
summary: Solve problems where a method calls itself, breaking complex tasks into simpler ones

sections.title: Recursion as Russian Dolls
sections.content: A Russian nesting doll (matryoshka) contains a smaller doll inside, which contains an even smaller doll, until you reach the tiniest doll that can't be opened. Recursion works the same way: a method calls itself with a smaller version of the same problem until it reaches a 'base case' so simple that no further recursion is needed. Every recursive function needs two things: a base case that stops the recursion (like the smallest doll), and a recursive case that calls itself with a simpler version of the problem. Without a base case, you get infinite recursion and a StackOverflowError.

sections.title: Calculating Factorial Recursively
sections.content: This method calculates n! using recursion: the classic example of divide-and-conquer

sections.title: Recursion vs Iteration: Trade-offs
sections.content: Any problem solved with recursion can also be solved with a loop. So why use recursion? Some problems are naturally recursive — like traversing a file directory tree, searching a family tree, or parsing nested mathematical expressions. For these, the recursive solution is shorter, clearer, and matches the problem's structure. The trade-off is performance: each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack, consuming memory. For deep recursion (thousands of levels), iterative solutions are safer. A good rule: if the problem involves nested structures or branching, reach for recursion; if it's linear, use a loop.

sections.title: Forgetting the base case or making it unreachable
sections.content: Most common mistake: writing a recursive method where the base case is never reached, causing infinite recursion. For example, calling factorial(-1) when the base case only checks n == 0. Fix: always ensure every possible input eventually reaches a base case. Wrong: public int fib(int n) { return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); } (no base case). Correct: add if (n <= 1) return n; before the recursive call.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a recursive method called sumDigits that takes a positive integer and returns the sum of its digits. For example, sumDigits(1234) should return 10 (1+2+3+4). Hint: the last digit is n % 10 and the remaining number is n / 10.
hints: What operation extracts the last digit of a number?
What operation removes the last digit?
What is the simplest possible input (base case) for this method?
